description: Rich, tactile language emphasizing textures, scents, and physical sensations to create immersive product descriptions that appeal to multiple senses simultaneously.
structural_recipe: Use adjectives and verbs that evoke touch (soft, silky, plush), smell (perfumed, fragrant, aromatic), and visual texture (glossy, luminous, dewy). Pair with emoji markers (♥️, ✨, 👄). Include bilingual Arabic-English descriptions. Emphasize ingredient benefits alongside sensory experience. Structure as benefit statement + sensory descriptor + natural ingredient callout.
why_it_works: Saudi audiences respond strongly to holistic sensory narratives that emphasize luxury, natural ingredients, and self-care ritual. Sensory language creates emotional connection and mental simulation of product experience. Arabic-English code-switching signals premium positioning. Appeals to beauty consciousness and wellness values prominent in Gulf market.
transplantation_caveats: Sensory language may feel over-embellished in non-luxury contexts or male-skewed categories
Ingredient transparency claims require regulatory compliance in KSA market
Fragrance references must align with halal/modest consumption values in some segments
cultural_constraints: Scent descriptions should emphasize natural/halal ingredients
Texture language should align with Islamic beauty concepts of natural enhancement
Bilingual execution (Arabic-English) strengthens credibility in premium beauty segments
